title: Te Pūtaiao o Te Awa: The Living Science of Our River | Te Pūtaiao o Te Awa: Te Pūtaiao Ora o Tō Tātou Awa
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will explore the traditional mātauranga and modern science of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the eel-filled stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. This awa was traditionally a vital food source and gathering place for Ngāti Kahungunu, with tuna (eels) representing both physical sustenance and spiritual significance. Students will connect with local kaumātua to understand how our tūpuna monitored water quality through observation of ngā tohu (natural signs) and how we can blend this knowledge with modern scientific methods to protect our awa for future generations.
learningObjectives: Measure and analyze at least three water quality parameters (pH, turbidity, temperature) using scientific instruments and compare with traditional observation methods
Explain the life cycle of tuna (freshwater eels) and their cultural significance to Ngāti Kahungunu through creation of a detailed biological and cultural profile
Design and implement a practical action plan to address one identified environmental issue affecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Present findings to peers and community members using both scientific terminology and te reo Māori concepts

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa | Connecting to Our River
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua who share pūrākau (traditional stories) and historical significance of the awa. Students make initial observations and sketches of the environment, noting native flora and fauna while learning traditional place names and their meanings.

activities.title: He Tohu Māori, He Tohu Pūtaiao | Māori Indicators, Scientific Indicators
activities.description: Working in small groups, students use water testing kits to measure scientific parameters (pH, dissolved oxygen, turbidity) while simultaneously recording traditional indicators of water health observed by Ngāti Kahungunu (presence of certain insects, water clarity, plant health). Students create comparative data tables analyzing both knowledge systems.

activities.title: Te Hīkoi a te Tuna | The Journey of the Eel
activities.description: Students research the extraordinary migration cycle of tuna (freshwater eels) from New Zealand to Tonga and back. They create detailed life cycle diagrams incorporating both biological stages and cultural significance, interviewing local knowledge holders about traditional harvesting practices and conservation methods.

activities.title: Te Rongoā me te Tiaki | Healing and Caring
activities.description: Based on their findings, student groups identify one environmental challenge facing the awa (e.g., sediment runoff, nutrient pollution) and develop an action plan combining traditional restoration methods (planting native riparian species used by Ngāti Kahungunu) with modern scientific approaches. Students implement a small-scale restoration project.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Water quality testing kits (pH meters, turbidity tubes, thermometer)
NIWA's Freshwater Fish Database and identification guides
'Te Rongoā Māori: Traditional Māori Healing' by Rob McGowan for plant medicinal properties

tasks: Scientific field report comparing traditional and scientific water quality assessment methods
Multimedia presentation on tuna biology and cultural significance
Action plan proposal for awa restoration with implementation evidence
Community presentation sharing findings with whānau and local iwi representatives

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Understanding about science, Investigating in science
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems, Interacting systems
NZC Principles: Cultural diversity, Community engagement, Sustainability
